note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses. Never rewrite, summarize, or reorder. Block windows are MEASURED onsets (FFT cross-correlation of each per-step mp3 against the joined master, 8 kHz mono PCM, rfft correlation): the nominal Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula overshoots the probed master (197.068s) by 0.446s (> 0.3 tolerance) because ffprobe's mp3 durations overstate the decoded sample counts. The measured onsets land exactly at Sigma(decoded_audio+0.4) with a clean 0.400s inter-block gap confirmed at every boundary. Master ffprobe duration 197.068s.

phrases.text: Open Challenges under Gamification.
This is the Challenge Board.
WashCentral turns your everyday targets into a running competition here,
with points on the line and a deadline on every one.
It's what keeps a crew pushing when the lobby goes quiet.
Start at the top.
Five numbers sum up the whole program at a glance:
how many challenges exist, how many are live, how many finished,
how many are about to start, and how many slipped past their deadline.
One row, and you know where the program stands.
Glance down the Gamification group.
Six pages sit together: the board you're on,
plus the leaderboard, targets, prizes, a recognition feed, and pulse surveys.
Today we stay on Challenges.
Just know the rest of the program lives one click away.
Now the board itself, Active and Upcoming Challenges.
Three controls sit up top: Active, All, and Completed.
They only change what you're looking at, never the data underneath.
And that green New Challenge button?
Leave it alone today.
Building challenges is a manager's job.
Take the first live card.
A ring shows how far along the team is.
Beside it, the reward in points, who it's for, when it ends, and how many days are left.
Then the hard number: washes done against the goal.
That's the whole story of the challenge in one glance.
Look lower on the same card.
A cluster of avatars and a headcount tell you who's taking part.
Off to the right, the current leader and their score.
So a technician can answer two questions fast:
how's the team doing, and who's out front.
The other two live cards read exactly the same way.
One tracks upsell dollars, another tracks attendance.
Only the metric and its unit change from card to card.
Learn one card and you've learned them all.
Below the live ones, the challenges warming up.
Same shape, but instead of progress you get a start date and the rule for winning.
Each one carries an Edit button.
That's a manager's control, so we read it and move on.
At the end of that row, a dashed tile invites you to create a new challenge.
It's the second door into the same builder.
We're reviewing, not building, so we leave it closed.
Now try the All view.
The board widens to every challenge, while the numbers up top hold steady.
Notice what didn't happen: nothing was edited or sent.
You're just changing the lens.
Switch to Completed.
Here are the challenges that already wrapped,
and the count lines up with the Completed figure you read at the top.
This is where you'd look back to see how a past competition landed.
Click back to Active.
The live and upcoming cards return, right where they were.
Three views, one board, and switching between them never touches a thing.
So that's Gamification.
The KPIs count the whole program,
the Active view shows what's live and what's coming,
and every card reads the same once you know the pattern.
When you need the wider picture,
the leaderboard, targets, prizes, recognition, and surveys are right there in the sidebar.
Read the board, point people to it, and leave the building to the managers.
